meta.researchNote: Facts in each resource's 'research' list were added from published outside sources on 2026-07-02; every fact carries its citation URL and was checked against the live source before inclusion.

topics.name: Designing my course
topics.blurb: I'm building or revising a course, help me write learning outcomes that are actually measurable and design for all my students from the start.
startHere: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test

topics.name: Assessment & grading
topics.blurb: How do I design assessments that match my outcomes, resist AI shortcuts, and grade them fairly and consistently?

topics.name: Active & engaging teaching
topics.blurb: practical ways to get students doing, talking, and collaborating in class, and to keep stressed learners able to engage at all.

topics.name: Presence, online & tech
topics.blurb: how to show up as a real, reachable instructor in an LMS-mediated course, setting up the shell, communicating consistently, and sustaining connection (including with students under stress) across Online/Blended/HyFlex/Onsite delivery.
startHere: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

topics.name: My feedback & growth
topics.blurb: I got my SFI / SGIF / peer-observation results back, how do I read them honestly and turn them into a better course (without the bad comments wrecking me)?
startHere: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

topics.name: Indigenizing my teaching
topics.blurb: practical, respectful ways to weave Indigenous knowledge, perspectives, and ways of knowing into course content, delivery, and assessment, without tokenism.

topics.name: Growing as a teacher
topics.blurb: Who am I as a teacher, and how do I keep getting better at it? The reflective foundation under everything else in ITL/ETaL.

topics.name: AI (cross-cutting)
topics.blurb: How do I use, and let students use, Generative AI in my course without breaking academic integrity?

resources.title: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
resources.oneLine: A one-page RDP infographic laying out a six-step reflective cycle for instructors to start applying Universal Design for Learning in their teaching.
concepts: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) as a course-design lens
A six-step iterative improvement cycle (Reflect, Identify, Investigate, Teach, Reassess, Reflect)
Starting small by targeting one UDL principle or checkpoint at a time
Reflective, question-driven teaching practice
Linking a chosen checkpoint to observed student needs and outcomes
useWhen: planning a new course with accessibility in mind from day one
rethinking a lesson that keeps losing part of the class
resources.poly: The "Teach a lesson... what does this look like in my teaching environment?" and reassess-by-demonstrated-skills steps map naturally onto RDP's applied, hands-on classrooms, labs, and shops, instructors can pilot a UDL checkpoint in a practical/skills demonstration and judge it by whether students performed the competency, not just recalled content.
resources.ai: Not addressed in the resource. A light tie-in: instructors could use AI to brainstorm UDL-aligned methods at the "Investigate" step (e.g., generating alternative ways to present material or let students demonstrate skills), keeping the RDP Faculty AI Framework's verification expectations in mind.
related: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

research.fact: CAST released UDL Guidelines 3.0 in July 2024. The update names 'learner agency' as UDL's goal and adds emphasis on learners' identities, belonging, and addressing barriers rooted in biases and systems of exclusion.
research.source: CAST, About the UDL Guidelines 3.0 Update (2024)

research.fact: The foundational UDL book, Meyer, Rose, and Gordon's Universal Design for Learning: Theory and Practice (2014), can be read free online through CAST's Clusive platform after creating a free account.
research.source: CAST, Read UDL: Theory and Practice for Free in Clusive

research.fact: A 2023 systematic review and meta-analysis of 13 studies from six countries, spanning K-12 and higher education, found overall positive effects for UDL-based teaching, while noting results varied considerably across studies.
research.source: Almeqdad et al., Cogent Education (2023)

research.fact: A 2025 study tracking 2,473 learners across 87 institutions for 24 months found that high-fidelity UDL implementation narrowed the achievement gap between marginalized and non-marginalized students by 34%; students with disabilities gained 42% versus 12% under low-fidelity implementation.
research.source: Guo & Wang, Scientific Reports (2025)

resources.title: Alignment Between Assessments and Outcomes
resources.oneLine: A one-page reference chart that pairs each of Bloom's six cognitive levels with example assessments and learning activities suited to that level, so instructors can align what they assess with their stated outcomes.
concepts: Constructive alignment between outcomes and assessments
Bloom's six cognitive levels (Remembering to Creating)
Matching assessment type to the cognitive demand of an outcome
Activity menus per level (e.g. simulation for Applying, debate for Analyzing)
Higher-order levels call for creation/judgement tasks, not recall tests
Source adapted from Carnegie Mellon University
useWhen: checking that a test actually measures your stated outcomes
matching assessment types to Bloom levels while planning a unit
resources.poly: The Applying and Creating rows already lean applied (prototyping, simulation, performance, lab work), a perfect place to attach RDP's hands-on/industry framing by swapping generic examples for trade/program-authentic tasks (e.g., a shop fabrication, a clinical sim, a client-brief project) so alignment is shown with real polytechnic deliverables.
resources.ai: This chart is a natural hook for assessment redesign in the GenAI era: which lower-order tasks (recall quizzes, summaries) are now easily produced by AI and pushing toward higher-order Create/Evaluate tasks that demand human judgement, plus noting where AI can be a legitimate tool within an activity. Ties to the Faculty AI Framework / Module 0.

research.fact: John Biggs coined the term "constructive alignment" in a 1996 Higher Education article (vol. 32, pp. 347-364), arguing that outcomes, teaching activities, and assessment should all target the same student performances. The publisher's page shows over 2,800 citations.
research.source: Biggs, Higher Education (1996), Springer

research.fact: Carnegie Mellon's Eberly Center, the source this chart adapts, warns that misaligned courses can undermine both student motivation and learning, for example when class time builds analysis skills but the exam rewards only factual recall.
research.source: Eberly Center, Carnegie Mellon University

research.fact: The level names on today's Bloom's charts come from a 2001 revision by Anderson and Krathwohl, which switched the labels from nouns to verbs and replaced "Synthesis" with "Create" as the highest level.
research.source: Centre for Teaching Excellence, University of Waterloo

research.fact: In a 2024 open-access study, ChatGPT drafted a constructively aligned graduate course (outcomes, weighted assessments, and a schedule), cutting planning time, but instructors still had to customize the plan because the AI output lacked classroom-specific context.
research.source: Pereira et al., International Journal of Educational Technology in Higher Education (2024)

resources.title: Authentic Assessment: Purposes and Rationale
resources.oneLine: A CTLS one-pager explaining what authentic assessment is, why it prepares students for real-world and AI-augmented work, with discipline-by-discipline examples and practical tips for getting started.
useWhen: redesigning an assignment students could shortcut with AI
making an assessment feel like real work in your field
resources.poly: Strong natural fit: the resource's core argument is preparing students for their 'professional context after school' through discipline-specific, real-world tasks, working with local organizations and businesses. RDP's applied/hands-on/industry framing attaches directly to the 'What is the Real World?' section and the discipline examples table (Nursing care plans, Business marketing plans, CS app builds) which can be reframed around RDP's program-to-industry pathways, work-integrated learning, and employer partnerships.
resources.ai: Central and explicit: a dedicated 'Authentic Assessments and Artificial Intelligence' section argues authentic assessment is AI-resilient because real-world tasks (building prototypes, collaboration, complex problem-solving) cannot be completed by AI alone, while preparing students to work alongside AI. It points readers to the RDP CTLS SharePoint page on the topic. Ties to the Faculty AI Framework / Module 0. Note: one source is an uncited ChatGPT response (OpenAI, 2023), which the upcoming refresh will address.
related: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
concepts: Authentic assessment as real-world, discipline-specific performance tasks vs. traditional exams
Target transferable skills: critical thinking, communication, collaboration, problem solving
Discipline-specific examples for designing authentic tasks
Authentic assessment as AI-resilient (tasks AI cannot do alone)
Start small: one authentic assessment rather than overhauling the whole structure
Matching assessment type strategically to the intended learning outcome
Emphasizing process over product; local/community application; intrinsic motivation

research.fact: Grant Wiggins' 1990 ERIC digest is a foundational text for authentic assessment: it argues assessment is 'authentic' when it directly examines student performance on worthy intellectual tasks, rather than inferring learning from proxies such as multiple-choice tests.
research.source: Wiggins, "The Case for Authentic Assessment," ERIC Digest ED328611 (1990)

research.fact: A systematic review of nearly three decades of research (1988-2015) distilled authentic assessment into three design dimensions (realism, cognitive challenge, and evaluative judgement) and proposed a step-by-step model instructors can use to build these into a course.
research.source: Villarroel et al., Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education, 43(5) (2018)

research.fact: In November 2023 Australia's higher-education regulator, TEQSA, published "Assessment reform for the age of artificial intelligence" (Lodge, Howard, Bearman and Dawson), arguing generative AI has intensified existing assessment challenges and guiding institutions on harnessing AI's potential while mitigating its risks.
research.source: Lodge, Howard, Bearman & Dawson, Assessment Reform for the Age of Artificial Intelligence, TEQSA (2023); record via Monash University

research.fact: The University of Bath's teaching centre publishes a free guide to designing authentic assessment, with worked examples (such as engineering students designing a campus rainwater-harvesting system with client feedback) and a caution that generative AI can assist with parts of a project but cannot coordinate the whole task.
research.source: University of Bath Learning and Teaching Hub, "An Introduction to Authentic Assessment"

resources.title: Bloom's Taxonomy Affective Domain
resources.oneLine: A single-page Red Deer Polytechnic reference chart listing measurable verbs (and verbs to avoid) for writing learning outcomes across the five levels of Bloom's affective domain.
useWhen: writing outcomes about attitudes, values, or professionalism
assessing things like teamwork or ethical practice
resources.poly: The affective domain is where RDP's applied/industry framing lands hardest: trades, health, and human-services programs need graduates who hold professional values, ethics, safety attitudes, and client-care dispositions, not just knowledge. This chart helps instructors write assessable outcomes for the workplace-readiness and professionalism behaviours employers expect.
resources.ai: Generative AI can draft affective-domain outcomes from these verbs (e.g., turning a desired professional attitude into a measurable Valuing- or Organizing-level statement), but a human must verify the verb truly captures an observable, assessable behaviour rather than a vague aspiration.
concepts: Affective domain measures internalization of attitudes, behaviours, and values
Five ascending levels: Receiving, Responding, Valuing, Organizing, Characterizing/Internalizing
Each level pairs a plain-language descriptor with measurable action verbs
Measurable verbs make learning outcomes assessable
Some verbs (appreciate, be aware of, understand) are too vague to measure and should be avoided
Visiting/volunteering are learning activities, not outcomes

research.fact: The affective taxonomy comes from Krathwohl, Bloom and Masia's 1964 Handbook II (David McKay), which defined the domain as emphasizing 'a feeling tone, an emotion, or a degree of acceptance or rejection' and paired each level with methods for assessing it.
research.source: Krathwohl, Bloom & Masia (1964), via SERC, Carleton College

research.fact: The University of Illinois Chicago teaching centre's free guide (2023) supplies objective-writing verbs for all three Bloom domains (cognitive, affective and psychomotor) and notes that as students move up the affective levels they become more self-reliant and internally motivated.
research.source: University of Illinois Chicago, Center for the Advancement of Teaching Excellence (2023)

research.fact: A 2025 scoping review of 20 studies across 13 countries found that values, ethics and emotional skills are essential for students' professional readiness, yet while teaching strategies are well documented, there is a significant gap in methods for assessing them.
research.source: Potgieter, Filmalter & Maree, Nurse Education in Practice (2025), open access

research.fact: A 2020 review of 22 studies on community-based medical education found attitudes and values develop mainly through role modelling and mentoring, applying knowledge in practice, and immersion in local communities, with structured reflection central, because affective growth is less visible than other learning.
research.source: Pagatpatan, Valdezco & Lauron, Medical Teacher (2020)

resources.title: Bloom's Taxonomy Cognitive Domain
resources.oneLine: A reference chart of measurable action verbs organized by the six revised levels of Bloom's cognitive (knowledge/intellectual) domain, plus a list of vague verbs to avoid when writing learning outcomes.
useWhen: picking the right verb for a learning outcome
levelling the questions on a quiz or exam
resources.poly: RDP's applied/hands-on mandate attaches at the upper levels (Applying, Creating) and the verbs that operationalize them, Demonstrate, Operate, Construct, Design, Develop, Assemble, which map directly to lab, shop, clinical, and trades competencies where students must do, not just describe; instructors writing outcomes for industry-aligned, competency-based programs can use this verb bank to make hands-on performance assessable.
resources.ai: When prompting an AI tool to draft or critique course learning outcomes, this verb bank gives the rubric: ask the AI to use only measurable verbs from the appropriate cognitive level and to flag any of the "verbs to avoid" (understand, appreciate, know) it produces, a quick guardrail against vague AI-generated outcomes.
concepts: Six revised cognitive levels: Remembering, Understanding, Applying, Analyzing, Evaluating, Creating
Each level pairs with measurable action verbs for writing learning outcomes
Top three levels (Analyzing, Evaluating, Creating) measure higher-order/critical thinking
Revised 2002 levels mapped to original 1956 terms (Knowledge, Comprehension, Application, Analysis, Synthesis, Evaluation)
A list of vague, unmeasurable verbs to avoid (understand, appreciate, know, value)

research.fact: The 2001 revision added a second "knowledge dimension" (factual, conceptual, procedural, and metacognitive) alongside the six cognitive levels, so any outcome can be classified by what students know and what they do with it. Krathwohl's 2002 overview article explains both dimensions with worked examples.
research.source: Krathwohl, D. R., Theory Into Practice, 41(4), 212-218 (2002)

research.fact: Anderson and Krathwohl renamed the levels from nouns to verbs to shift attention from possessing knowledge to actively performing it, and made Create (formerly Synthesis) the top level. The University of Waterloo's Centre for Teaching Excellence keeps a free explainer with sample outcomes and linked verb lists.
research.source: University of Waterloo, Centre for Teaching Excellence, Bloom's Taxonomy tip sheet

research.fact: A 2020 open-access study compared 47 published Bloom's verb lists and found little agreement between them: four verbs (select, explain, relate, arrange) appeared at every level. The authors provide a free consensus "master list" plus a list of ambiguous verbs to avoid in learning outcomes.
research.source: Newton, Da Silva & Peters, Frontiers in Education (2020)

research.fact: A 2022 analysis of 940 biology assessment questions found that a verb alone is a poor guide to cognitive level (verbs like "describe" and "identify" appeared across several levels), so classify a question by the whole task it demands, not just its opening verb.
research.source: Larsen et al., CBE-Life Sciences Education (2022)

resources.title: Bloom's Taxonomy Psychomotor Domain
resources.oneLine: A one-page RDP/CTLS reference chart listing the progressive levels of Bloom's psychomotor (physical-skill) domain with measurable action verbs for writing learning outcomes.
useWhen: writing outcomes for hands-on skills in labs, shops, or clinicals
grading physical technique fairly
resources.poly: Strongest of the three Bloom charts for RDP: the psychomotor domain is exactly where polytechnic, hands-on programs live, trades, nursing, technologies, apparel, and labs. The chart explicitly invokes the journeyman/apprentice model at the Coaching level, so it maps directly onto RDP's skills-demonstration and competency-based assessment in shops, clinics, and studios.
resources.ai: Minimal direct AI relevance. AI can help instructors draft and vet measurable psychomotor outcomes or generate level-appropriate verb suggestions, but physical-skill performance is inherently human-demonstrated and not AI-assessable, making this a useful contrast point in AI-and-assessment discussions.
concepts: Psychomotor domain measures physical skills, movement, coordination, precision and technique
Six progressive levels: Observing, Modeling, Recognizing Standards, Correcting, Applying, Coaching
Each level supplies measurable action verbs for writing learning outcomes
Lower levels (sensory observation) feed into higher judgment and standard-setting levels
Outcomes should use measurable verbs and avoid vague, unmeasurable ones
Coaching/applying levels mirror trades apprenticeship (journeyman-to-apprentice instruction)

research.fact: Three educators later built psychomotor taxonomies to fill the gap: Simpson (1966, 1972), Dave (1970), and Harrow (1972). The University of Illinois Chicago teaching centre's guide names all three and uses Simpson's version in its outcome-writing guidance.
research.source: University of Illinois Chicago, Center for the Advancement of Teaching Excellence (2023)

research.fact: Simpson's psychomotor taxonomy has seven levels: perception, set, guided response, mechanism, complex overt response, adaptation, and origination. The University of Connecticut publishes a free one-page chart pairing each level with sample verbs and hands-on examples such as calibrating tools and repairing a faucet.
research.source: University of Connecticut, Learning Taxonomy: Simpson's Psychomotor Domain

research.fact: Dave's 1970 taxonomy tracks five stages of skill learning: imitation (copying others), manipulation (working from instructions), precision, articulation, and naturalization. The University of New England's assessment office offers a free quick guide with suggested outcome verbs for every stage.
research.source: University of New England, Office of Assessment, Affective and Psychomotor Domains Quick Guide

research.fact: This area is still evolving: a 2022 paper in the European Journal of Dental Education proposed a new taxonomy for clinical psychomotor skill development, arguing that virtual-reality and haptic simulators call for updated frameworks for teaching hands-on skills.
research.source: Perry, Bridges & Burrow, European Journal of Dental Education (2022)

resources.title: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
resources.oneLine: A CTLS guide explaining what a Continuous Appointment ePortfolio is at RDP, the required and optional artifacts probationary faculty must compile for their appointment hearing, and practical tips and platform options for building one.
useWhen: starting to plan your continuous appointment portfolio
deciding what evidence of your teaching to collect this term
resources.poly: The entire document is RDP-specific: it codifies Red Deer Polytechnic's own Faculty Performance Policy, the Continuous Appointment Hearing process, and RDP evaluation instruments (Peer Observation, SGIF, SFI), making the applied/institutional career-progression framing central rather than incidental.
related: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide
concepts: Continuous Appointment ePortfolio as a reflective digital collection for the appointment hearing
Mandatory artifact checklist (Retrospective Report, Dean/Associate Dean reports, Peer Observations, SGIF, SFI, CV)
Optional artifacts: teaching philosophy, course materials, professional development evidence
Platform selection and trade-offs (Sway, Google Sites, Wix, Weebly, WordPress)
Privacy/data-protection: never publish SFI-containing portfolios publicly
Organizing by probationary year and contextualizing each artifact
Links to CTLS templates and video/printable tutorials

research.fact: In 2016 the Association of American Colleges and Universities added ePortfolios as the eleventh high-impact practice, citing a decade of student-success evidence. The announcing editorial argues that a well-done portfolio can act as a 'meta' practice that amplifies the other ten.
research.source: Watson, Kuh, Rhodes, Penny Light & Chen, International Journal of ePortfolio 6(2) (2016)

research.fact: A systematic review of 56 studies across seven health professions in ten countries concluded that well-implemented portfolios increase personal responsibility for learning and support professional development, and that electronic versions encourage reflection better than paper ones.
research.source: Tochel et al., Medical Teacher, BEME Guide No. 12 (2009)

research.fact: AAEEBL, the international association for ePortfolio practice, publishes ten Digital Ethics Principles (version 3, 2022) with concrete strategies for accessibility, data responsibility, and author rights and re-use permissions, directly relevant when deciding what to share in a portfolio.
research.source: AAEEBL, Digital Ethics Principles in ePortfolios (2022)

research.fact: A 2023 open-access scoping review of 76 studies found that a portfolio's running record helps professionals spot their strengths, weaknesses, and learning needs and build an improvement plan, capturing workplace learning that one-off assessments miss.
research.source: Lim et al., Postgraduate Medical Journal 99(1174) (2023)

resources.title: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide
resources.oneLine: A click-by-click technical walkthrough for copying, renaming, populating, and publishing the CTLS-provided Google Sites template to build a Continuous Appointment ePortfolio.
useWhen: building your ePortfolio site step by step
moving your collected artifacts into the CTLS template
resources.poly: The whole document is RDP-specific: it builds the institution's own Continuous Appointment ePortfolio using the CTLS template, names RDP roles (Academic Director, Dean, Continuous Appointment Committee) and RDP's evaluation instruments (Peer Observation, SGIF, SFI), and ends by sharing the link with the CA Committee for review. The applied/hands-on framing attaches naturally to encouraging a variety of artifact types (videos, presentations, PowerPoints) that demonstrate real teaching practice.
related: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
concepts: Copying a view-only Google Sites template via the Drive 'Make a copy' action and renaming the site
Pre-compiling teaching artifacts into a dedicated Google Drive folder before building
Populating the prescribed CA ePortfolio page structure (Home/About Me, CV, Teaching Philosophy, Year One/Two, Supporting Documents)
Adding content two ways: paste text into the About Me area, or insert a PDF/Word doc from Drive
Using Content Blocks and the Insert menu to add images and text
Managing pages, subpages, and reordering via the Pages panel; setting a homepage
Publishing the Google Site and sharing the link with the Continuous Appointment Committee for review

resources.title: Delivering Planning Checklist
resources.oneLine: A CTLS operational checklist of priority and recommended tasks for instructors to complete before, during, and after a course, broken out by delivery mode (Online, Blended, HyFlex, Onsite).
useWhen: setting up your course before the semester starts
doing a mid-course or end-of-course sweep for loose ends
resources.poly: The delivery-mode matrix (Online/Blended/HyFlex/Onsite) maps directly onto how RDP runs applied, industry-aligned programs across labs, shops, and remote cohorts; the Course Facilitation Record feeding the Renewal phase reflects the polytechnic emphasis on continuously tuning a course to current practice.
resources.ai: No AI content present. A natural hook: the online testing-integrity items (Lockdown Browser/Monitor) and feedback-timeline tasks are exactly the touchpoints where the Faculty AI Framework now reshapes practice, assessment authenticity in the age of generative AI, and AI-assisted feedback/announcements to sustain instructor presence.
related: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
concepts: Course delivery lifecycle (before / first week / throughout / last week)
Delivery-mode differentiation: Online, Blended, HyFlex, Onsite
Priority vs. recommended task triage
Blackboard course setup: course copy, outline upload, grade center, weighted totals, grading schema
Setting expectations: response windows and feedback timelines
Maintaining instructor presence and netiquette monitoring
Online testing integrity (Lockdown Browser / Monitor)
Course closeout: SFI reminder, final grades, Course Facilitation Record for Renewal phase

research.fact: The Quality Matters Higher Education Rubric, Seventh Edition (launched July 2023) is a widely used course-review standard with 8 General Standards and 44 Specific Review Standards, and it applies to online, hybrid, and HyFlex courses.
research.source: Quality Matters, Higher Education Rubric, Seventh Edition (2023)

research.fact: A review of 39 studies found that learning-management-system activity data predicted student course outcomes with about 72% accuracy on average; in one demonstration, data from just the first four weeks identified 74% of at-risk students before their first major exam.
research.source: Arizmendi et al., Behavior Research Methods (2023)

research.fact: Hybrid-Flexible Course Design (2019) by Brian Beatty of San Francisco State University is a free, openly licensed (CC BY) book offering implementation strategies, worksheets, and institutional case studies for HyFlex classes where students choose between in-person and online participation.
research.source: Beatty, Hybrid-Flexible Course Design, EdTech Books (2019), via Open Textbook Library

research.fact: The 2025 CHLOE 10 survey of U.S. chief online learning officers found nearly 9 in 10 institutions plan to expand online programs, yet only 28% of faculty are rated fully prepared for online course design and 45% for online teaching.
research.source: Quality Matters, Eduventures & EDUCAUSE, CHLOE 10 Report (2025)

resources.title: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
resources.oneLine: A one-page CTLS infographic orienting instructors new to online teaching, covering Blackboard course design tips, recommended communication hardware, and strategies for connecting with students online.
useWhen: teaching online for the first time
finding out what the CTLS can take off your plate
resources.poly: The hardware recommendations (document cameras, headsets, speaker/mics) attach naturally to RDP's hands-on, demonstration-heavy polytechnic teaching, where instructors need to show physical objects, equipment, or worked procedures live to remote students.
related: What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra
Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching
concepts: Flexible Blackboard course design (assignments, resources, weekly chunking, announcements)
Recommended communication hardware for online teaching (mic, headset, document camera)
Online connection strategies: clear course menus and weekly content videos
Creating a dedicated student Q&A discussion forum
Collaborate Ultra for virtual office hours and Live Classroom
Consistent primary/secondary communication channels with students
How to reach the CTLS for support (Teams, email, SharePoint resources)

research.fact: The Community of Inquiry model, introduced by Garrison, Anderson and Archer in 2000, holds that meaningful online learning happens where three elements overlap: teaching presence, social presence and cognitive presence.
research.source: Garrison, Anderson & Archer, The Internet and Higher Education (2000)

research.fact: A 2022 meta-analysis pooling 19 studies found teaching presence (how visibly an instructor designs, guides, and joins in a course) strongly linked to student satisfaction (r = .51) and moderately linked to actual learning (r = .35) in online and blended courses.
research.source: Martin, Wu, Wan & Xie, Online Learning, 26(1) (2022)

research.fact: A ready-made survey measuring how students experience teaching, social, and cognitive presence in a course is free to use and adapt under a Creative Commons licence. Validated in 2008 by Arbaugh, Garrison, Richardson, Swan and colleagues, it comes in several languages.
research.source: The Community of Inquiry (Athabasca University and partners), CoI Survey

resources.title: Rubrics: Purposes and Rationale
resources.oneLine: A CTLS tip-sheet explaining what rubrics are, the three main rubric types, design strategies, and step-by-step Blackboard how-tos for building and grading with rubrics.
concepts: Rubrics specify assessment criteria and levels of achievement to standardize grading and feedback
Three rubric types: analytical (grid), single-point (strengths/improvement around a meets-expectations column), and holistic (overall snapshot)
Design strategies: use generators (iRubric, RubiStar), align criteria to learning outcomes, use observable/measurable language, revisit purpose with students
Blackboard how-tos: create via Course Tools, edit the rubric grid, copy and edit, associate with an assessment, grade with a rubric
Rubric scoring options: No Points, Points, Point Range, Percent (criteria must total 100%)
Rubrics aren't always the right fit; sometimes transitioning away (reducing dependency) is appropriate

anchors.label: Is a rubric always a good fit?
How to edit the rubric grid
Attach a rubric to an assessment

useWhen: building your first rubric for an assignment
cutting grading time on a written assessment
explaining grades that students keep disputing
resources.poly: The applied/industry angle attaches at criterion design: RDP's hands-on, competency-based programs (trades, health, technology) lend themselves to observable, measurable performance criteria, and the note about reducing rubric dependency in upper-year discipline courses speaks directly to scaffolding learners toward professional-judgement standards used in industry.
resources.ai: Generative AI could draft rubric criteria and level descriptors from a learning outcome and assignment brief, suggest observable/measurable wording to replace subjective language, and help convert between analytical, single-point, and holistic formats, extending the resource's existing nod to rubric generators (iRubric, RubiStar, 'Rubric Wordsmith').
related: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test

research.fact: A review of 21 studies found that sharing rubrics with students can improve performance by making assessment expectations transparent, which in turn can reduce student anxiety; rubrics can also aid feedback use, self-efficacy, and self-regulated learning.
research.source: Panadero & Jonsson, Educational Research Review (2013)

research.fact: A 2023 meta-analysis of 23 studies found rubric use had a moderate positive effect on students' academic performance (effect size g = 0.45 after correcting for publication bias), plus smaller positive effects on self-regulated learning and self-efficacy.
research.source: Panadero, Jonsson, Pinedo & Fernández-Castilla, Educational Psychology Review (2023)

research.fact: Assessment expert Susan Brookhart defines a rubric as 'a coherent set of criteria for student work that describes levels of performance quality', and cautions that rubrics are commonly misunderstood and misused.
research.source: Brookhart, How to Create and Use Rubrics for Formative Assessment and Grading (ASCD, 2013)

research.fact: The American Association of Colleges and Universities offers 16 free, faculty-developed VALUE rubrics for essential outcomes such as communication and analytical skills; they have been downloaded more than 860,000 times and used at over 3,000 colleges and universities.
research.source: AAC&U, VALUE Rubrics

resources.title: Strong Start - Self-Enroll Instructions
resources.oneLine: A 3-page, screenshot-driven walkthrough showing how to self-enrol via the LOOP and Blackboard into the "A Strong Start - Top 8 Tips and Tools" resource site.
useWhen: pointing a struggling student at semester-start strategies
prepping advice for the first weeks of term
resources.poly: Weak fit - this is pure LMS access mechanics with no applied/industry pedagogy. The only hook is that the destination 'Strong Start - Top 8 Tips and Tools' site promises practical, get-your-semester-on-track strategies, consistent with RDP's hands-on, just-in-time support ethos.
related: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
concepts: Logging into the LOOP with RDP credentials
Navigating to Blackboard from the LOOP
Using a direct organization URL to reach the Strong Start site
Clicking through the seven self-enrolment steps (enroll, submit, OK)
The 'A Strong Start - Top 8 Tips and Tools' resource site as the destination

resources.title: Teaching Philosophy
resources.oneLine: A CTLS guide explaining what a teaching philosophy is, why it matters at Red Deer Polytechnic, and a step-by-step process for drafting, sharing, and revising one.
useWhen: writing or refreshing your teaching philosophy statement
preparing your year-end self-reflective report or portfolio
resources.poly: The RDP-specific framing is explicit: the teaching philosophy is a required element of RDP's Continuous Appointment Review and feeds the Year-End Faculty Self-Reflective Report; instructors are encouraged to use concrete classroom examples from their applied teaching context and consult a CTLS Learning Designer.
resources.ai: No AI content. A natural hook: instructors could use an AI tool to brainstorm or pressure-test draft beliefs against the six guiding questions, but the resource stresses authentic, first-person voice and human (colleague) feedback, which sets a useful guardrail against over-relying on AI-generated prose.
related: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide
concepts: Teaching philosophy as a reflective, action-oriented narrative (beliefs to action)
Beliefs - Goals - Actions - Results - Reflect cycle
Institutional uses: Continuous Appointment Review, teaching portfolio/ePortfolio, awards, job applications
Year-End Faculty Self-Reflective Report tie-in
Teaching Perspectives Inventory (TPI) as a self-reflection tool
Faculty Focus six guiding questions for drafting
Essence statement (1-2 sentence distillation)
Iterative practice: share with colleagues/students, seek feedback, revise over time
Avoiding jargon and writing authentically in first person

research.fact: The classic template for these statements is Nancy Van Note Chism's POD Network essay, which recommends five components: how you think learning happens, how you teach, your goals for students, how you put the philosophy into action, and a personal growth plan.
research.source: Chism, N.V.N., "Developing a Philosophy of Teaching Statement," Essays on Teaching Excellence 9(3), POD Network (1997-98)

research.fact: The Teaching Perspectives Inventory remains free to take online at teachingperspectives.com. Its 45 questions profile your teaching across five perspectives (Transmission, Apprenticeship, Developmental, Nurturing, and Social Reform), with sub-scores showing whether each rests on your beliefs, intentions, or actions.
research.source: Pratt, D.D. & Collins, J.B., Teaching Perspectives Inventory

research.fact: The TPI is a validated instrument, not just a quiz: a study marking its first decade analyzed data from more than 100,000 respondents in over 100 countries and confirmed the inventory's reliability and validity as a self-report measure of teaching perspectives.
research.source: Collins, J.B. & Pratt, D.D., Adult Education Quarterly (2011)

research.fact: For a Canadian model, the University of Calgary's Taylor Institute offers a free, openly licensed guide that structures the statement as a 1-2 page first-person narrative with four parts (beliefs, strategies, impact, and goals) and links to sample statements from Calgary instructors.
research.source: Kenny, N., Taylor Institute for Teaching and Learning, University of Calgary (2015)

resources.title: Trauma Informed Pedagogy
resources.oneLine: A Red Deer Polytechnic CTLS handout explaining what trauma is, how it impairs learning, and offering concrete trauma-informed teaching practices for supporting students and instructors themselves.
useWhen: adjusting a course when the class is carrying visible stress
building predictability into a course students find overwhelming
resources.poly: The applied/industry angle attaches to the heavy practicum and clinical placements common at a polytechnic, where students under high contact-hour loads and workplace stress benefit from predictable routines, flexible absence policies, and scaffolded reminders; RDP's own Counselling Services, the linked RDP Trauma Responsive course-design resource, and instructor workload realities (manageable grading for large applied classes) ground it locally.
related: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching
A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
concepts: Trauma as a protective response, not the event itself (and its variability across individuals)
How trauma impairs memory, decision-making, focus, time management, and engagement
Predictability and reduced cognitive load via consistent routines, tools, and checklists
Flexibility in assessment, absence policies, and modes of meeting students
Instructor presence, empathy, and compassion (arrive early, regular check-ins, small-group coffees)
Scaffolding learning and reminders to compensate for stress-impaired memory
Community building and sense of belonging (safe spaces, optional discussion boards, gratitude docs)
Empowerment through student voice and choice; authentic/alternative assessment over high-stakes exams
Overlap with Universal Design for Learning (UDL)
Instructor self-care and use of campus/provincial mental-health supports

anchors.label: How can I support my students?

research.fact: These practices align with SAMHSA's 2014 framework from the U.S. Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration, which defines a trauma-informed approach by four assumptions (realize, recognize, respond, and resist re-traumatization) plus six principles including safety, trustworthiness and transparency, and empowerment, voice, and choice.
research.source: SAMHSA, SAMHSA's Concept of Trauma and Guidance for a Trauma-Informed Approach (2014), hosted by NCTSN

research.fact: The term "trauma-informed educational practice" was introduced by Carello and Butler in a 2015 article that adapted trauma-informed care principles to teaching, offering concrete guidelines for applying the principle of safety in the classroom.
research.source: Carello & Butler, Journal of Teaching in Social Work, 35(3), 262-278 (2015), via ERIC

research.fact: Canadian data show the need: in the Spring 2022 National College Health Assessment of 11,322 Canadian post-secondary students, 33% screened positive for serious psychological distress, and 52% said stress had hurt their academic performance in the past 12 months.
research.source: American College Health Association, NCHA-III Canadian Reference Group Executive Summary (Spring 2022)

research.fact: In October 2020 Canada released the world's first national standard for post-secondary student mental health and well-being (CSA Z2003:20), a voluntary, flexible framework from the Mental Health Commission of Canada and CSA Group that institutions can use to guide campus-wide supports.
research.source: Mental Health Commission of Canada, National Standard for Post-Secondary Students (2020)

resources.title: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching
resources.oneLine: A short CTLS handout on how trauma affects student learning and concrete ways to design and teach online/LMS courses through a "trauma lens."
useWhen: organizing your LMS shell so stressed students can navigate it
choosing content and deadlines with student load in mind
resources.poly: Attaches through the explicitly RDP-branded supports and tools: the Blackboard Online Course Template, CTLS Learning Designers as a hands-on help channel, RDP Counselling Services, and Alberta provincial hotlines, grounding the abstract "trauma lens" in concrete, institution-specific resources an RDP instructor can use immediately.
related: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra
A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
concepts: Trauma-responsive / trauma-informed course design (the 'trauma lens')
How trauma impairs cognition: memory, concentration, decision-making, persistence
LMS organization for predictability (Blackboard Online Course Template, consistent navigation)
Reducing cognitive load when selecting content (flag key concepts, critical video segments)
Accessible activity instructions (breakout-room directions in the chat box)
Student mental-health supports and referral pathways (Counselling Services, Alberta 211, Mental Health Hotline)

anchors.label: Strategies for your LMS and course design

research.fact: Mays Imad's 2020 essay 'Leveraging the Neuroscience of Now' explains that under trauma the brain's emotional limbic system hijacks its reasoning cortex, and offers seven teaching strategies: first safety, then trust built through clear, transparent, reliable communication about coursework.
research.source: Mays Imad, Inside Higher Ed (2020)

research.fact: The advice to reduce cognitive load rests on Cognitive Load Theory: all new information must pass through a working memory with strictly limited capacity before it can be stored. Sweller and colleagues review the theory's design implications in a free, open-access 2019 article.
research.source: Sweller, van Merriënboer & Paas, Educational Psychology Review (2019)

research.fact: The free open textbook Humanizing Virtual Learning (University of Waterloo, Trent University and Conestoga College, via eCampusOntario) cites estimates that 60-85% of postsecondary students have experienced trauma, and shares five trauma-informed principles for online facilitation: predictability, connection, flexibility, safety and self-awareness.
research.source: Humanizing Virtual Learning, eCampusOntario Pressbooks

research.fact: A peer-reviewed 2023 article offers free rubrics for auditing asynchronous online courses through a trauma-informed lens, rating consistency, clarity and flexibility - for example, keeping course structure uniform and embedding assignment instructions as text, video and/or audio.
research.source: Moses, Bayne & Moore, Journal of Applied Instructional Design 12(2) (2023)

resources.title: What is Pedagogy?
resources.oneLine: A short CTLS primer defining pedagogy as the theory and practice of teaching, framing education as the interplay of curriculum, environment, and pedagogy, and pointing faculty to RDP's ITL/ETaL development programs.
useWhen: getting your bearings before other teaching development work
choosing which CTLS program to join next
resources.poly: RDP's applied/industry framing attaches through andragogy's emphasis on real-world applicability and self-directed learning, and through the named RDP teaching-development pathways (ITL/ETaL), pedagogy here is positioned as the practical "how" that makes applied curriculum teachable.
related: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
concepts: Pedagogy as the theory and practice of teaching and learning (an umbrella term)
The three interacting domains of education: curriculum (what), environment (where), and pedagogy (how)
Effective pedagogy is as important as well-designed curriculum and a suitable environment
Andragogy (adult learning, Knowles) and its overlap with learner-centred pedagogy
Heutagogy and the pedagogy/andragogy/heutagogy continuum
RDP teaching development pathways: Introduction to Teaching and Learning (ITL) and Excellence in Teaching and Learning (ETaL)
Pedagogy adapts to online delivery and to the physical/social context of learners

research.fact: Stewart Hase and Chris Kenyon coined the term heutagogy in a 2000 peer-reviewed article, defining it as "the study of self-determined learning": the learner, not the teacher, decides what to learn and how. The original article is free to read via the Internet Archive.
research.source: Hase & Kenyon, "From Andragogy to Heutagogy," ultiBASE, RMIT (2000; archived copy)

research.fact: Blaschke's 2012 open-access review (IRRODL, vol. 13, no. 1) traces heutagogy's roots in andragogy and shows how it builds learner "capacity and capability" for complex workplaces, a free, citable starting point for instructors who want more depth.
research.source: Blaschke, International Review of Research in Open and Distributed Learning (2012)

research.fact: A 2025 open-access review of 22 peer-reviewed studies found heutagogical approaches improve learner engagement, autonomy, and capability, notably in medical education, and notes AI tools now make self-determined learning easier to support in practice.
research.source: Panta, Cureus (2025), via PubMed Central

research.fact: William & Mary's teaching centre distils Knowles' andragogy into practical terms: adults are self-directed, bring life experience as a foundation for learning, and want problem-centred material they can apply quickly; some K-12-style practices can cause adult learners to disengage.
research.source: Studio for Teaching and Learning Innovation, William & Mary

resources.title: What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra
resources.oneLine: A practical CTLS guide to designing and facilitating effective small-group breakout activities, covering group roles, deliverables, observation, debriefing, and when NOT to use breakouts.
useWhen: planning small-group work in a live online class
fixing breakout activities that students treat as dead air
resources.poly: The structured-roles and shared-deliverable model maps directly onto RDP's applied, team-based shop/lab/clinical work, breakout roles mirror real workplace team functions (lead, recorder, QA/equity check), so framing breakouts as rehearsal for industry teamwork lands well.
resources.ai: Not addressed in the resource. A natural AI hook: using a generative-AI assistant to draft breakout activity instructions, role cards, or debrief prompts, and AI-aware deliverables (e.g., having groups critique or fact-check AI output in the shared doc).
related: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
concepts: Assigning structured group roles (facilitator, note-taker, reporter, timekeeper, equity monitor, questioner/devil's advocate)
Requiring a shared-doc/slides deliverable to give activities focus and accountability
Pre-forming groups via LMS group tools rather than random/re-formed groups
Active observation with a pre-declared instructor role (participant, observer, corrector)
Report-out debrief to build accountability and move the whole class forward
When NOT to use breakouts: large classes, inconsistent use, cold random groups

research.fact: In a meta-analysis of 225 studies, students in lecture-only STEM courses were about 1.5 times more likely to fail than students in active learning courses (33.8% vs. 21.8% failure rates), and active learning raised exam performance by roughly 6%.
research.source: Freeman et al., PNAS (2014)

research.fact: The structured-roles advice rests on David and Roger Johnson's cooperative learning research: groups succeed when tasks build in positive interdependence (no one succeeds unless everyone does) and individual accountability, so no one can 'hitch-hike' on others' work.
research.source: Cooperative Learning Institute, "What is Cooperative Learning?"

research.fact: In a 2023 study of 664 higher education students, breakout rooms boosted perceived learning through purposeful interpersonal interaction, and students' technology readiness and sense of group identity predicted that interaction, so who you group together matters, not just the task.
research.source: Wilkins et al., International Journal of Educational Management (2023)

research.fact: Cornell's Center for Teaching Innovation offers concrete sizing guidance: keep breakout groups small (2-4 people) so everyone gets airtime, give each participant at least 1-2 minutes to share, and plan 5-15 minutes per breakout for most tasks.
research.source: Cornell University Center for Teaching Innovation, "Zoom breakout room tips"

tasks.label: Write learning outcomes
tasks.intro: From picking the level to checking the assessment will actually measure it.

steps.note: pick the level, then the verb
add if the outcome is about attitudes or professionalism

tasks.label: Build a rubric

steps.note: types, benefits, and step-by-step build
make the criteria trace back to outcomes

tasks.label: Make an assessment AI-resistant
tasks.intro: Assessments grounded in real, discipline-specific work are the hardest to shortcut. The AI lens on the home screen shows where AI fits in every resource.

steps.note: ground the task in real work from your field
raise the cognitive demand past recall

steps.note: work the before-the-course checklist for your delivery mode

steps.res: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching
steps.note: make the structure predictable, so finding things is never the hard part

tasks.label: Support stressed students

steps.note: practices for planning and teaching

steps.res: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching
steps.note: the online and LMS version

steps.res: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
steps.note: design the flexibility in, so fewer students have to ask for it

tasks.label: Start my ePortfolio
tasks.intro: For continuous appointment: what goes in it, the philosophy that anchors it, and a template to build on.

steps.res: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
steps.note: what it is and what to include
write the philosophy that anchors it

steps.res: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

tasks.label: Build a course from scratch
tasks.intro: The order to do it in: the design lens, then the outcome, then the thing you mark it with.

steps.res: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
steps.note: design for the whole class before you design for the average one
pick the level, then the verb the outcome will use
check the assessment actually measures the outcome you wrote
build the thing you will mark it with, before you have to mark it
the last pass before the course goes live

tasks.label: Find my feet as a new instructor
tasks.intro: The vocabulary the rest of this assumes, a design lens to start with, and the one document you will be asked for later.

steps.note: the vocabulary everything else here takes for granted

steps.res: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
steps.note: a six-step cycle you can start applying to one lesson this week
start a draft now; you will need it at continuous appointment

tasks.label: Design for every student in the room
tasks.intro: Accessibility and trauma-informed practice built in at the design stage, rather than retrofitted when someone is already struggling.

steps.res: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) janet test
steps.note: build the flexibility in up front

tasks.label: Cut my grading time
tasks.intro: Most slow marking was designed in long before the marking started.

steps.note: a lot of slow marking is an assessment that was never aimed at the outcome
the biggest lever: decide what you are looking for before you look
tasks worth marking, and harder to fake

glossary.def: Behaviour giving an unearned or unfair academic advantage; unauthorized AI use falls under it at RDP.
An evaluation document written by the Academic Director, included as a supporting ePortfolio artifact.
sources: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: The branch of Bloom's Taxonomy covering attitudes, emotions, values, and feelings rather than knowledge or physical skill.
Software that flags AI-generated work; its use must be disclosed in the Course Outline beforehand.
Provincial 24/7 confidential mental-health crisis and referral services instructors can point students to.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

glossary.def: A grid rubric with criteria down the side and achievement levels across the top, scored criterion by criterion for detailed feedback.
The theory and practice of teaching adults, emphasizing purpose, self-directed learning, and real-world relevance (Knowles).
The Blackboard feature for posting important course messages to all students.
sources: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: A piece of evidence of learning placed in a portfolio, such as an assignment, video, presentation, or practicum record.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: The Blackboard feature that lets students submit work online for grading.
sources: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: Evaluating learning through realistic, discipline-specific tasks that mirror professional practice rather than abstract tests.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

glossary.def: Learning organized around real-world, complex problems rather than abstract exercises.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: The learning management system (LMS) RDP uses to host online courses, assignments, and content.
sources: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: An RDP-provided standard course shell giving students a predictable, consistent layout to navigate.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

glossary.def: A tiered framework classifying learning objectives by increasing complexity across cognitive, affective, and psychomotor domains, used to write and scaffold outcomes.
RDP's institution-level strategic outcome statements that course work ultimately ladders up to.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: A temporary small-group space split off from the main online session, each with its own audio, video, whiteboard, and chat.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching
Using Breakout Rooms in Collaborate Ultra: Best Practices
What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra

glossary.def: An institutional document from the Associate Dean specifying the approved curriculum elements for a course.
Highest affective level, a value is so embedded it consistently guides behaviour and defines the person's character.
A specific, actionable teaching suggestion nested under a broader UDL principle or guideline.
sources: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL)

glossary.def: The category of Bloom's Taxonomy covering knowledge and intellectual (thinking) skills, as opposed to feelings or physical skills.
The mental effort a task demands; reducing it via routines and highlighting essentials frees capacity for learning.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

glossary.def: The bottom-right Collaborate Ultra menu opening Chat, Attendees, Share Content, and My Settings.
sources: Collaborate Ultra General Overview: Best Practices

glossary.def: Blackboard's web-based virtual-classroom tool for live, synchronous online classes (now retired at RDP).
sources: Collaborate Ultra General Overview: Best Practices
New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help
Using Breakout Rooms in Collaborate Ultra: Best Practices

glossary.def: A diagram showing how ideas relate, used to assess understanding rather than rote recall.
Designing assessments and activities so their type and cognitive demand match the stated learning outcome.
A view of learning in which learners actively build their own understanding rather than passively receiving it.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: A pre-formatted layout chunk in Google Sites for adding combinations of images and text.
sources: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: Advance notes flagging potentially triggering media so students can prepare.
RDP's equivalent of tenure/permanent status, granted after a successful probationary review for which a teaching ePortfolio is required.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: A Blackboard function that duplicates content from a previous or Master course site into a new course shell.
A document where instructors log delivery notes and course flags to inform the course Renewal phase.
The navigation sidebar of a Blackboard course that organizes links to content, tools, and modules.
sources: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: The official course document where AI-use rules and detection-tool disclosures should be stated.
Red Deer Polytechnic's Centre of Teaching, Learning and Scholarship, which supports faculty teaching, design, and technology.
sources: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide
New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: A required, concrete output that gives a group activity focus and accountability.
sources: What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra

glossary.def: A group role that deliberately challenges ideas to provoke deeper, more robust discussion.
sources: What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra

glossary.def: A camera (e.g., IPEVO VZ-R) that captures physical pages or objects on a desk to share live during online teaching.
sources: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: An Equity, Diversity, Inclusion and Indigenization statement often requested alongside a teaching philosophy in job applications.
Early Learning and Child Care program, cited for its sample academic-integrity statement on AI.
An organized digital collection of work samples and reflections that documents learning, growth, and achievement over time.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide
Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos
Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: A group role whose job is to ensure every member gets a chance to speak.
sources: What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra

glossary.def: A 1-2 sentence distillation capturing the core of your teaching beliefs (not a summary of the longer document).
RDP's official policy documents defining evaluation requirements and ePortfolio contents for continuous appointment.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos

glossary.def: Low-stakes assessment used during learning to give feedback and guide improvement, as opposed to summative assessment of final achievement.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT) that produce new text, images, or other content in response to a prompt.
A pre-built, view-only website skeleton you copy to your own Google account and fill with your own content.
sources: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: Blackboard's gradebook, where a weighted total column calculates final grades by applying percentage weights to assessments.
The Blackboard rule set that maps numeric scores to letter grades or other displayed grade values.
The study of self-determined learning, where learners drive what and how they learn; the step beyond pedagogy and andragogy.
An AAC&U-designated teaching practice shown to deepen learning and improve retention; ePortfolios are the 11th.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: More complex cognitive work (analyzing, evaluating, creating) beyond recall and basic understanding.
A rubric grouping several criteria into one overall judgment, giving a big-picture snapshot rather than criterion-by-criterion scores.
A delivery mode where students can choose to attend in person, online synchronously, or online asynchronously.
Technical interoperability standards supporting import/export of portfolio data between platforms (not universally supported).
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: The process by which a learner takes on a value or attitude until it becomes part of how they consistently act.
Doing a task for internal interest/satisfaction versus for external rewards like grades.
An ePortfolio used for job applications, interviews, or promotion (e.g., RDP's Continuous Appointment ePortfolio).
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: A trades training relationship where an experienced worker instructs a learner, cited as the psychomotor 'Coaching' level.
An approach organizing teaching around learners' needs, preferences, and active participation rather than content delivery alone.
A CTLS specialist who helps instructors develop course design, assessment, and teaching practice (including a teaching philosophy).
A statement of what a student should be able to do by the end of instruction, written with a measurable action verb.
The columns in a rubric (e.g., Novice, Competent, Proficient) describing degrees of quality for each criterion.
The Collaborate Ultra session link placed in a course menu where students join real-time class meetings.
sources: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: The online platform (at RDP, Blackboard) where course content, activities, and grades are organized.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

glossary.def: An LMS feature (in Blackboard) for pre-assigning students to persistent groups.
sources: What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra

glossary.def: Exam-integrity tools (Respondus) that restrict a student's browser and, with Monitor, record them via webcam during online tests.
sources: New to Online Teaching? The CTLS is Here to Help

glossary.def: The shared file area of the main session where breakout-room files must be saved to persist after groups close.
sources: Using Breakout Rooms in Collaborate Ultra: Best Practices

glossary.def: An action word naming observable, assessable behaviour (e.g., list, analyze, demonstrate) rather than an internal state like 'understand'.
Thinking about one's own thinking and learning process; a skill ePortfolio reflection is meant to develop.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: The session role (typically the instructor) with rights to share content and create, monitor, and join breakout groups.
sources: Collaborate Ultra General Overview: Best Practices
Using Breakout Rooms in Collaborate Ultra: Best Practices

glossary.def: Norms of respectful, professional communication and conduct in online/digital course spaces.
A student-support resource provided through Blackboard Organizations to help learners succeed online.
A non-credit Blackboard site (vs. a course) used to host resources or communities, accessed via an /organizations/ URL.
The student prioritizes and reconciles competing values, building them into a coherent system.
The theory and practice of teaching and learning, what educators do, what they ask students to do, and how they assess learning.
A documented classroom observation of a faculty member by a colleague, used as evidence of teaching practice.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: An assessment where students apply skills to produce or do something realistic (prototype, simulation, plan) rather than recall facts.
A Blackboard rubric scoring option allowing a range of values per achievement level rather than a single fixed point.
One of UDL's three core areas, multiple means of engagement, representation, and action/expression, that organize its guidelines and checkpoints.
sources: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL)

glossary.def: The typically 3-year window during which new continuous-contract faculty are evaluated before an appointment decision.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos

glossary.def: Valuing how students create and revise (drafts, ongoing reflection) rather than only the final output; also the portfolio distinction between record-of-learning and curated showcase.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: The area of Bloom's Taxonomy dealing with physical skills, movement, and hand-eye coordination.
Making the built site live and shareable via link; the site can still be edited after publishing.
sources: Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: A status button letting a participant signal they want to speak without interrupting.
sources: Collaborate Ultra General Overview: Best Practices

glossary.def: The step of gathering evidence of whether a newly tried strategy actually helped students demonstrate knowledge or skills.
sources: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL)

glossary.def: Lowest affective level, the student simply becomes aware of an attitude, behaviour, or value.
The psychomotor level where learners identify the criteria that define correct performance of a skill.
An ePortfolio focused on showing learning, growth, reflection, and development rather than finished products.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: A stage in RDP's course lifecycle where a delivered course is reviewed and updated for future offerings.
Bringing groups back to the whole class to share their work, providing accountability and a chance to build on it.
sources: What Nobody Tells You About Designing Effective Breakout Room Activities in Blackboard Collaborate Ultra

glossary.def: The student actively reacts to or engages with a value, e.g., participates or complies.
A self-authored reflective summary the candidate writes covering their probationary period, included in the ePortfolio.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: The updated taxonomy that renamed levels as verbs/gerunds and swapped the top two so 'Creating' sits above 'Evaluating'.
A grading tool laying out assessment criteria and the levels of achievement for each, used for both scoring and feedback.
A plagiarism-detection tool; students must be told before assignments are due that it (or an AI detector) will be used.
Breaking a complex task into supported steps that build on prior knowledge so learners gain skill and confidence gradually.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: A learner's belief in their own capability, treated as a prerequisite for motivation.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: Adding yourself to a Blackboard course/organization site rather than being added by an administrator.
The degree to which students are metacognitively, motivationally, and behaviourally active participants in their own learning.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: The top-left Collaborate Ultra menu for reporting issues, getting help, viewing the privacy policy, and leaving the session.
sources: Collaborate Ultra General Overview: Best Practices

glossary.def: RDP's formal end-of-course student evaluation survey of a course/instructor.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: A facilitated mid-course process gathering structured student feedback on teaching; a built-in ePortfolio artifact.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos
Creating your Continuous Appointment ePortfolio with CTLS's Google Sites Template: Step-by-Step Guide

glossary.def: An ePortfolio type that highlights a person's best completed work.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: How-Tos

glossary.def: A setting that randomly redistributes participants across breakout groups.
sources: Using Breakout Rooms in Collaborate Ultra: Best Practices

glossary.def: A three-column rubric stating only the 'meets expectations' standard in the middle, with space to note strengths and areas to improve on either side.
Aggregated SFI score reports at the Class, Department Collective, and College Collective levels for comparison.
sources: Continuous Appointment ePortfolios at RDP: Rationale and How-Tos

glossary.def: RDP's policy (revised Aug 21, 2023) defining academic misconduct and related procedures.
A free online quiz that surfaces your beliefs/perspectives about teaching to prompt reflection.
A short reflective narrative stating what you believe about teaching and learning and how you enact it, with concrete examples.
The model that education is an interaction of three harmonizing parts: curriculum (what), environment (where), and pedagogy (how).
RDP's web portal/intranet that staff and students log into with their RDP credentials to reach internal services.
Conventional testing formats like multiple-choice exams, essays, and quizzes with predetermined correct answers.
Mezirow's idea that critically examining and revising one's habitual assumptions reshapes one's perspective.
sources: Teaching with ePortfolios at RDP: Purposes and Rationale

glossary.def: The body's protective response to a harmful or life-threatening event (not the event itself), experienced uniquely by each person.
A perspective for reviewing course design to spot changes that restore capacities trauma takes away from learners.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

glossary.def: Teaching that anticipates the prevalence and effects of trauma and adjusts course design and instruction to reduce barriers to learning.
sources: Trauma Responsive Online and LMS Course Design and Teaching

glossary.def: A framework for designing flexible courses with multiple means of engagement, representation, and action/expression so they reduce barriers and work for all learners by default.
sources: A Process for Getting Started: Universal Design for Learning (UDL)

glossary.def: The student recognizes a value's worth and shows commitment to it through their choices.
Giving students agency over how they engage and how their learning is measured, fostering autonomy.
An annual RDP reflective report where faculty critique the year and set goals, informed by their teaching philosophy.
